title: India-UAE Energy Alliance: Strategic Crude Oil Storage and LPG Supply Framework
lede: Prime Minister's May 2026 UAE visit yielded three major energy agreements between Indian and Emirati state oil firms, establishing cross-border crude storage capacity and long-term liquefied petroleum gas supply chains.

sections.context: On 15 May 2026, India's Prime Minister concluded a visit to the UAE that produced a suite of energy-sector memoranda of understanding focused on strategic petroleum reserves and hydrocarbon supply. The most significant outcome was an MoU between Indian Strategic Petroleum Reserves Limited (ISPRL) and Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC) establishing potential crude oil storage arrangements: ADNOC would be permitted to store up to 30 million barrels in India's strategic reserves, including facilities at Vishakhapatnam in Andhra Pradesh and new reserve capacity under development at Chandikol, Odisha. Reciprocally, India would utilize crude oil storage capacity in Fujairah, UAE, as part of its own strategic petroleum reserve. A second agreement between Indian Oil Limited (IOCL) and ADNOC addresses liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) supply, establishing a framework for long-term LPG sale and purchase agreements through ADNOC Gas Limited. A third outcome—a Framework for Strategic Defence Partnership—was also announced but the source text does not elaborate on its specific objectives.
sections.threads: This energy-focused outcome extends a longer trajectory of India-UAE strategic deepening. [[india-ae-pms-eighth-uae-visit-in-12-years-signals-energy-led-str-2026|The Prime Minister's eighth UAE visit in twelve years has consistently prioritized energy cooperation]], reflecting India's critical import dependence and UAE's position as a reliable Gulf supplier. The crude oil storage arrangements in particular signal recognition of regional security vulnerabilities; [[india-ae-india-condemns-fujairah-attack-reaffirms-west-asia-diplomac-2026|recent attacks on UAE energy infrastructure]] have underscored the strategic value of distributed reserve capacity. The LPG agreements complement broader [[india-ae-india-uae-strategic-energy-and-investment-deepening-5-b-2026|India-UAE strategic energy and investment frameworks]], which have been accelerated following high-level bilateral visits. Domestically, these agreements operationalize India's strategic petroleum reserve infrastructure—facilities in coastal Andhra Pradesh and Odisha—as part of a regionalized energy security posture.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor three implementation timelines. First, the materialization of crude storage in Chandikol, Odisha, and the extent to which ADNOC commits barrels to Indian facilities versus alternative regional hubs. Second, the negotiation and execution of the long-term LPG purchase agreement between ADNOC Gas and IOCL—the framework is agreed but commercial terms remain to be finalized. Third, the content and scope of the Defence Strategic Framework, which the source text describes incompletely. The reciprocal arrangement—Indian use of Fujairah storage—carries particular significance given the Strait of Hormuz's strategic chokepoint status and recent regional turbulence; this arrangement may signal India's intent to diversify crude sourcing and storage away from single-point-of-failure vulnerabilities. Over the next 12-18 months, watch for announcements of actual barrel commitments, LPG price discovery mechanisms, and any public detail on the defence framework.
